rows.refusal_boundary: micro kernels should refuse only when bounded opcode or memory support is missing rather than silently degrading into unrelated learned behavior
rows.note: bounded micro-kernel family where compiled exactness and recurrent runtime baselines are both meaningful frontier anchors

rows.refusal_boundary: branch-heavy kernels should refuse when control-flow support falls outside the explicit bounded runtime family instead of silently flattening into dense replay
rows.note: control-heavy kernel family used to separate bounded control-flow pressure from open-ended search

rows.refusal_boundary: memory-heavy kernels should refuse when bounded state or address support is exceeded rather than hiding that pressure behind aggregate exactness
rows.note: dense load/store kernel family that exposes memory pressure separately from search pressure

rows.refusal_boundary: long-loop kernels should refuse when recurrent or halting budgets are exhausted instead of silently treating long horizons as small-prefix work
rows.note: loop-heavy kernel family used to anchor recurrent-budget frontier questions

rows.refusal_boundary: CLRS bridge workloads should refuse broad promotion until the bridge stays explicit about trajectory family, length bucket, and exact bounded export coverage
rows.note: shared shortest-path bridge family that keeps CLRS-style trajectory work legible without claiming full algorithm-benchmark closure

rows.refusal_boundary: search-heavy Sudoku workloads should refuse rather than degrade when verifier/search traces or exact compiled support are unavailable
rows.note: real search family where search-specific traces and exact compiled closure matter more than flat dense replay

rows.refusal_boundary: matching workloads should refuse when bounded exact search or compiled support is missing rather than pretending parallel frontier traces imply full executor closure
rows.note: matching family that mixes parallelizable frontier structure with exact combinatorial search pressure

rows.refusal_boundary: module memcpy workloads should refuse when module-scale lowering or export coverage is missing instead of being collapsed into kernel-only exactness
rows.note: module-scale copy family used to anchor low-depth but non-kernel module execution

rows.refusal_boundary: module parsing workloads should refuse when bounded control-flow or state channels are missing instead of being over-read from memcpy-style wins
rows.note: module parsing family used to separate nested control and state pressure from flat copy kernels

rows.refusal_boundary: module checksum workloads should refuse once bounded memory-span assumptions break instead of silently widening module exactness
rows.note: module checksum family that remains low-depth but nontrivial at module scale

rows.refusal_boundary: vm-style module workloads should refuse when dispatch or call-frame support leaves the bounded family instead of being treated like flat parsing or checksum cases
rows.note: module-scale dispatch family with the highest current module-control pressure in the public suite

support_boundaries: the taxonomy is a research-only workload-classification and frontier vocabulary over the current benchmark pack; it does not promote served capability or flatten compiled, learned, search, and fast-path work into one generic score
budget descriptors are regime labels for the current workload families, not a proof that every workload with the same label is solved or benchmarked
preferred lanes remain refusal-first guidance; unsupported or under-mapped regions must stay explicit instead of being smoothed into aggregate capability claims

claim_boundary: this report is a research-only capability-frontier map over the current workload taxonomy and committed Tassadar artifacts. It keeps compiled exactness, recurrent runtime closure, verifier-guided search, and learned shared-depth architecture distinct, and it keeps under-mapped or refusal-first regions explicit instead of flattening them into one generic executor score
summary: Current workload frontier covers 11 families and 26 preferred-lane observations, with 7 under-mapped preferred lanes across 7 workload families and 5 refusal-first workload families kept explicit.
